Next.js EaseMotion Template

A comprehensive guide and reference example for integrating EaseMotion CSS smoothly in the Next.js App Router environment.

Integration Blueprint

4 CHECKS COMPLETED
🌐
Step 1

Global Configuration

Import `easemotion.css` in `/app/layout.jsx` to make standard CSS animations globally accessible to both Server and Client Components.

💅
Step 2

Token Customization

Override design variables (e.g. `--ease-color-primary` or `--ease-speed-medium`) inside `/app/globals.css` to align the theme with your brand.

🛡️
Step 3

Hydration Guards

Use standard React hooks (`useState`, `useEffect`) and portals to prevent Server-Side Rendering (SSR) hydration warnings on dynamic elements like modals.

⚡
Step 4

Animation Classes

Apply pure CSS classes (like `ease-hover-lift` or `ease-fade-in`) directly in JSX `className` fields. Fully compatible with static extraction.

Next.js Setup Code Snippet

Import the core package styles globally inside your App Router layout file to enable layout-stable, zero-CLS entrance states:

// app/layout.jsx
import React from 'react';
import './globals.css';
import 'easemotion-css/easemotion.css';
export default function RootLayout({ children }) {
  return (
    <html lang="en">
      <body>{children}</body>
    </html>
  );
}

Interactive Transition List

Add and remove items to see EaseMotion entrance animations (`ease-fade-in` + `ease-slide-up`) in response to dynamic state changes.

💡 Global layout bindings configured
⚡ Server-Side Rendering (SSR) compatibility verified
💧 Zero-CLS client hydration validated

💡 SSR Best Practice

Always run heavy animations or entrance state bindings on static classes during SSR. For interactive transitions (like dynamic modal portals or lists), ensure components mount on the client first using client-only checks to guarantee smooth, hydration-safe operation.

© 2026 EaseMotion CSS Project. Self-Contained Next.js Integration Template.
